    "military_operation": "Operation Overlord, Battle of Long Khanh",
    "operational_tactical_setting": {
      "mission_objectives": "Operation Overlord was a 'Search and Clear' operation aimed at destroying and disrupting enemy elements, specifically the 3/33rd North Vietnamese Army (NVA) Regiment, 274th (Main Force) Viet Cong (VC) Regiment, D445 VC Battalion, and local VC Units, to prevent them from launching attacks into northern Phuoc Tuy Province and threatening the 1st Australian Task Force (1ATF) Base at Nui Dat.",
      "terrain": "The operation took place in southern Long Khanh Province, close to the Phuoc Tuy Province border, and north-east of the De-Courtenay Rubber Plantation. The area was characterized by thick, tangled jungle and a massive hidden underground base system.",
      "enemy_situation": "Intelligence indicated that the 3/33rd NVA Regiment, 274th (Main Force) VC Regiment, D445 VC Battalion, and local VC Units (including 13 Chau Duc, C36 Sapper Company, and the K8 Support Company) were using the area to retrain, re-equip, and reinforce. These forces were actively launching attacks into northern Phuoc Tuy Province. The enemy was described as 'very good soldiers, very good fighters'.",
      "friendly_force_disposition": "Operation Overlord involved 1ATF and US Army Forces. The US Army's 2/8th Bn, 3 Brigade, 1st US Cavalry Division (Air Mobile) was deployed to block north-east and east along the Suoi Luc river. The 4RAR/NZ (ANZAC) Bn, which included V Company, was deployed to block south and south-east along the Suoi Ran river system. A Squadron, 3rd Cavalry Regiment, was used as a cordon, and 3RAR was tasked to search between the two blocking battalions. Operational Control was vested in the Commander 1ATF, whose Headquarters (Forward) was relocated to Courtenay Hill. Private Harding was serving in the Company Support Section, Victor 6 Company (V6), which was part of the 4th Royal Australian Regiment / New Zealand (ANZAC) Battalion (4RAR/NZ).",
      "narrative_summary": "Operation Overlord commenced on June 5, 1971, with the insertion of blocking forces, including Australian, New Zealand, and US troops. On June 6, 1971, 3RAR discovered a suspect bunker system. The following morning, June 7, 1971, B Company's 5 Platoon of 3RAR began an assault on enemy positions, encountering heavy automatic fire and becoming pinned down, marking the beginning of the Battle of Long Khanh. The fighting was intense, with Australian troops pinned down by heavy fire. Centurion tanks provided close support, crushing many bunkers. The enemy managed to slip away under the cover of night, leaving behind a large network of bunkers. The operation continued with rifle companies having fleeting contacts. On June 12, an Australian armoured personnel carrier was ambushed, resulting in seven deaths and several wounded. Operation Overlord concluded on June 14, 1971. Private Kenneth Horomai Harding was shot by a sentry while approaching the outskirts of a bunker on June 24, 1971, and died of his wounds later that night."

derived_details.circumstances_of_death: During Operation Toan Thang II, Private Wickman was killed in action when V Company was attacked by approximately 30 Vietcong in a jungle clash shortly after his unit had killed three Vietcong guerrillas. He sustained a gunshot wound to the head and neck.

      "narrative_summary": "Private Michael John Wickman, serving with V Company, 4 RAR/NZ (ANZAC) Battalion, was killed in action on June 22, 1968, during a contact with approximately 30 Vietcong. This engagement occurred shortly after his unit had killed three Vietcong guerrillas in a jungle clash a few miles from the Nui Dat Task Force Base. At the time, the 4 RAR/NZ (ANZAC) Battalion was participating in Operation Toan Thang II, which aimed to interdict enemy infiltration routes and rocket sites in the III Corps area. The New Zealand infantry companies, including V Company, were integrated with Australian battalions within the 1st Australian Task Force (1ATF) at Nui Dat, Phuoc Tuy province, and were involved in constant jungle patrols, ambushes, and cordon-and-search operations."
